description: Quality and correctness rules for offline single-file data-visualization mini-apps built with d3 (v7) and Chart.js (bar-chart races, zoomable sunbursts, chord diagrams, force graphs, brushed scatter plots, radial trees, live dashboards). Covers the classic UMD script-tag load contract (no ESM under file://), pinning versions, keeping all data INLINE (offline, no fetch), responsive SVG/canvas sizing with redraw on resize, Chart.js responsive:true + maintainAspectRatio:false, smooth transitions, accessible color/typography, and shipping a populated, interactive chart on first load. For d3/Chart.js dataviz mini-apps only; do not leak into games or productivity apps.

guidelines.rule: Load d3 and Chart.js with classic UMD <script src> tags that expose the globals d3 and Chart. NEVER use an ESM 'import * as d3 from "d3"' / 'import Chart from "chart.js"' / <script type=module>. ESM modules silently fail to load under file:// in the WKWebView preview, leaving an empty page.
guidelines.why: These mini-apps ship as static single-file bundles and run under file:// inside WKWebView, which is not a secure context. A bare module import or an importmap referencing d3/chart.js does not resolve there and the global is never defined. The UMD builds attach d3 / Chart to window, which works offline under file://.
examples: GOOD: <script src="https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/d3@7.9.0/dist/d3.min.js"></script> then a normal <script> using d3.select(...)
WRONG: import * as d3 from 'd3'; - ESM does not resolve under file:// in the preview

guidelines.rule: Pin specific versions in the CDN URLs (for example d3@7.9.0, chart.js@4.4.3). Code against d3 v7's flat namespace (d3.scaleLinear, d3.hierarchy, d3.forceSimulation) and Chart.js v4. The platform vendors the referenced version offline; pinning keeps the vendored copy matching the API you coded against.
guidelines.why: d3 v7 uses a single flat d3 namespace and module-as-microlibrary structure; older snippets using d3.scale.linear (v3) break. Chart.js v4 differs from v2/v3 (scales config, tree-shakeable in ESM but global in UMD). Pinning keeps the bundle deterministic and the API stable.

guidelines.rule: Keep ALL data INLINE in the file - a JS array/object literal embedded in the script. NEVER fetch data from a URL, d3.json/d3.csv a remote file, or load a remote dataset. The mini-app runs offline under file:// with no network, so any fetch fails and the chart stays empty.
guidelines.why: These bundles are offline single files. d3.json('https://...') or fetch() returns nothing under file:// (and even a relative file fails CORS in WKWebView), so a chart depending on a fetch renders blank. Embedding a curated sample dataset as a literal guarantees the visualization always has data.
examples: GOOD: const data = [ {name:'Alpha', value:42}, {name:'Beta', value:31}, ... ]; // inline literal
GOOD: a hierarchical literal for the sunburst/tree; an inline matrix for the chord diagram
WRONG: d3.json('data.json').then(draw) or fetch('https://api...').then(...) - empty offline

guidelines.rule: Size the SVG/canvas responsively to its container and REDRAW on resize. Read the container's width/height (clientWidth/clientHeight), set the SVG viewBox or width/height from it, and on window resize (and orientationchange) recompute scales and redraw. Use a debounced resize handler. Set body margin 0 so the chart fills the space.
guidelines.why: A hard-coded 960x500 SVG looks tiny on a phone and clipped on a wide screen, and the preview/hub render at many sizes. Reading the container size and redrawing keeps the chart full and legible everywhere and correct after rotation. viewBox plus preserveAspectRatio gives crisp scalable SVG.
examples: const w = container.clientWidth, h = container.clientHeight; svg.attr('viewBox', `0 0 ${w} ${h}`);
WRONG: svg.attr('width', 960).attr('height', 500) fixed with no resize handling

guidelines.rule: For Chart.js, configure responsive:true and maintainAspectRatio:false, and give each canvas a sized parent container (the parent controls the dimensions). This lets a chart fill its dashboard card and resize with the layout. Do NOT set fixed width/height attributes on the canvas itself.
guidelines.why: Chart.js with maintainAspectRatio:true (the default) forces a fixed aspect and ignores the parent height, producing a squished or overflowing chart in a grid. responsive:true + maintainAspectRatio:false + a sized wrapper is the standard way to make a Chart.js chart fill its card and reflow with the dashboard.
examples: new Chart(ctx, { type:'line', data, options:{ responsive:true, maintainAspectRatio:false } });
WRONG: <canvas width="600" height="300"> with maintainAspectRatio defaulting true

guidelines.rule: Use smooth transitions and animation for state changes. In d3 use selection.transition().duration(...) with d3.easeCubic and the general update pattern (join/enter/update/exit) so bars reorder, arcs tween, and points move smoothly. In Chart.js keep the built-in animations and use chart.update() for live data. Animate the bar-chart-race overtakes and the sunburst zoom with arcTween.
guidelines.why: The wow factor of these toys is the motion: bars racing and overtaking, sunburst arcs tweening on zoom, force nodes settling. Hard cut-overs look broken; tweened transitions look polished. The d3 enter/update/exit join is the correct way to animate data changes without orphaned elements.

guidelines.rule: Make the visualization interactive and tactile: hover/tap to highlight + show a tooltip, click to zoom/expand (sunburst, tree), drag nodes (force graph) or brush a region (scatter). Wire BOTH mouse and touch (pointer events) and keep hit targets reasonable. The visualization should respond to the very first interaction.
guidelines.why: A static chart is an image; the product is an interactive explorer. Tooltips on hover/tap, click-to-zoom, draggable force nodes, and brushable scatters are what make these toys engaging and worth opening. Pointer events cover touch so a phone user can explore too.

guidelines.rule: Choose readable, attractive color and typography. Use d3 scale-chromatic schemes (d3.schemeTableau10, d3.interpolateViridis/Warm, d3.schemeSet2) or a curated palette, ensure text contrasts with its background, size fonts so labels are legible on a phone, and add a title and a legend/axis labels so the chart is self-explanatory. Truncate or rotate long labels.
guidelines.why: Default black-on-white with tiny labels looks unfinished; a tuned categorical/sequential scheme plus clear titles and legends makes the chart beautiful and understandable. Legible fonts and contrast matter especially on mobile. d3-scale-chromatic ships well-designed schemes for free.
examples: add an <h1>/<text> title, axis labels, and a legend; rotate long x labels -45deg

guidelines.rule: Use the correct d3 layout/generator for each chart and follow its data shape: d3.hierarchy + d3.partition for sunburst, d3.tree/d3.cluster for trees, d3.chord + d3.ribbon for chord diagrams, d3.forceSimulation (forceLink/forceManyBody/forceCenter) for force graphs, d3.arc/d3.pie, d3.line/d3.area, d3.brush. Feed them the shape they expect (root.descendants(), a matrix, {nodes,links}).
guidelines.why: These layouts do the hard geometry; hand-rolling arc/ribbon/force math is error-prone and usually wrong. The common failure is feeding a layout the wrong data shape (a flat array to d3.hierarchy, or links referencing node objects vs ids) so it throws or renders nothing. Match the generator to its documented input.
examples: sunburst: const root = d3.hierarchy(data).sum(d => d.value); d3.partition().size([2*Math.PI, radius])(root);

guidelines.rule: On every redraw, clear or update via the join pattern - do NOT append duplicate SVG groups on each resize/update. Either remove the old content (svg.selectAll('*').remove() before a full redraw) or use a stable data join (selection.data(...).join(...)). For Chart.js, update the existing chart instance or destroy() it before creating a new one.
guidelines.why: A frequent bug is appending a fresh <g>/axis/chart on every resize so duplicates stack up, the SVG balloons, and tooltips fire multiple times. Clearing first or using join keeps one clean rendering. Creating a second Chart on the same canvas without destroying the first throws 'Canvas is already in use'.
examples: Chart.js resize: chart.resize() (built-in) - do not new Chart() again on the same canvas

guidelines.rule: Ship a populated, interactive chart on the FIRST load - never an empty axes-only frame or a chart that needs a button press to get data. Render the inline data immediately, run the entry animation/transition once on load, and have controls (play/pause, speed, zoom-out) enhance rather than gate the initial render.
guidelines.why: The render gate can pass an empty-but-loaded SVG. The product is a chart actually showing data. An empty force graph, a sunburst with no arcs, or a bar race paused at frame zero with nothing visible reads as broken. Render the data first; let controls refine it.
examples: on load: draw all marks from the inline data, then run the intro transition
bar race autostarts (or shows the first frame populated) with a pause button
WRONG: blank axes until the user clicks 'Load'

guidelines.rule: Clamp devicePixelRatio for canvas-based renders to <=2 and keep redraws efficient. For Chart.js set devicePixelRatio to Math.min(window.devicePixelRatio,2) (or rely on its responsive handling), debounce resize redraws, and avoid recomputing a force simulation from scratch on every frame. Keep live-dashboard update intervals reasonable (a few per second, not per frame).
guidelines.why: A 3x DPR canvas plus a busy live dashboard tanks mobile; clamping to 2 caps the pixel cost. Un-debounced resize redraws and per-frame full recomputes cause jank. A live dashboard updating a few times a second looks lively without burning the battery.
examples: debounce the resize redraw rather than redrawing on every resize event
